title: Animated rating star
design_intent: The design intent is to make the state change legible without adding decorative motion or layout instability.
reference_inspiration: No external reference is required; the interaction is specified by duration, easing, state change, and reduced-motion behavior in this row.
motion_reasoning: The motion should communicate the state change directly, stay under a short perceptual threshold, preserve layout stability, and provide a reduced-motion fallback.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A visual-only animation that ignores reduced motion, cleanup, focus, or layout cost can look fine in a demo while causing jank, inaccessible state changes, or orphaned timers/tweens in production.

title: Final: Motion as craft
design_intent: Animation is craft — invisible when good, painful when bad. Treat with same rigor as code.
reference_inspiration: Apple, Linear, Stripe — they invest in motion as first-class.
motion_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Every motion communicates — make it intentional.
2. Match easing/duration to emotional context.
3. Compositor-only properties: transform + opacity.
4. Reduced motion = graceful alternative, not absence.
5. Test on slow devices.
6. Build a motion system: tokens for duration, ease, spring.
7. Document the WHY behind each animation.
8. Audit periodically — motion that lost its purpose should be removed.
9. Motion is craft — treat with same rigor as the code that powers it.

performance_strategy.what_animates: transform + opacity by default; avoid layout properties

interaction_details: Every team member uses the same tokens
what_breaks_naive_approach: Motion as afterthought: inconsistent feel; performance issues; accessibility complaints. With discipline: motion becomes a competitive moat — apps feel premium, fast, considerate. Users may not articulate why, but they FEEL it.
